title: Market Stalls & Vendors
description: The vendors, stalls, and curiosities of a fantasy market — not just what's for sale but who's selling it, how they sell it, and why the thing they're selling exists at all.

entries.result: A spice merchant with a cloth-covered wagon whose stock is organized by warmth — mild on the left, 'not for the uninitiated' on the right — who offers samples from a personal spoon.
A used weapon dealer whose inventory is displayed blade-up in a barrel of sand and who has strong opinions about which items have good histories versus merely long ones.
A soap maker selling bars in a dozen varieties, each scented with something local — pine from the north valley, lavender from the abbey garden, smoke from the eastern mills.
A map seller whose maps are detailed and whose notes in the margins are sometimes worth more than the maps themselves.
A locksmith selling not just locks and keys but a guide to the common lock types of six regional cities, written and bound by hand.
A rope seller with every gauge, twist, and material in organized coils, who can recommend the right rope for any purpose and will give the wrong recommendation only once.
A pot mender doing repairs on the spot, a pile of bent and cracked cookware beside her, the tap-tap-tap of her work audible from two stalls away.
A vendor of dried mushrooms from seven different forests who can identify which mushroom is from which forest by smell and who has strong feelings about mushroom fraud.
An alchemy supply stall with common reagents labeled in three languages and a sign that reads 'we don't sell what you actually want and we don't know what you're talking about.'
A seedseller with varieties organized by growing season and a companion selling soil amendments three stalls down, the two of them coordinated in a way that suggests a long partnership.
A bookseller with a cart of used texts, organized by the seller's personal system, who will find any requested title if given a day and told it's important.
A cartographer's assistant selling printed city maps, regional maps, and one badly reprinted copy of a map that was supposed to be rare.
A vendor of travel rations — dense, caloric, flavorless — who maintains that survival and pleasure are different categories and respects both.
A cobbler doing repairs from a folding stool with a last between his knees, who quotes time and price instantly and is accurate on both.
A stall of second-hand clothing, organized by size and climate suitability, with a vendor who can estimate dimensions by eye and is right within one size.
A glassblower selling bottles, vials, lenses, and one extraordinary piece she made at 3am and isn't sure what to charge for.
A tallow candle seller whose candles are uniform, honest, and cheap, who becomes irritated when customers ask if they're magical.
A tool seller with hammers, chisels, saws, and drawknives laid out on a cloth, each oiled, each sharp, each priced with a paper tag the seller wrote at dawn.
A vendor of ink in sealed clay jars, each labeled with color and permanence rating, who also sells quills, scrapers, and the knowledge of how to use all of the above.
A cheese merchant with a block of every variety she carries, a knife, a clean cloth, and an uncomplicated belief that the product speaks for itself.
A perfumer with twenty vials on a cloth-covered tray and the ability to describe each one using language that makes people spend more than they intended.
A net mender and rope braider working two crafts simultaneously, selling fish nets, cargo nets, and one net of unusual gauge for unusual purposes.
A leather seller with hides sorted by thickness, tannage, and intended use, and a hand-written guide to choosing the right hide that he gives away to first-time buyers.
A vendor of preserved foods — pickles, dried fruit, salted fish, fermented vegetables — who has opinions about which preservation method is superior and is not quiet about them.
A mirror seller with inventory ranging from hand mirrors to full-length, each polished to show the buyer a more favorable angle than they usually see themselves from.
A medicine seller at the edge of the herbalist district, selling general remedies, common treatments, and a pamphlet on self-care that a physician endorsed and then regretted endorsing.
A vendor of hand-drawn playing cards in five different regional styles, plus a single complete deck in a style no one has identified.
A chalk seller with sticks in white, red, yellow, and a dark gray that makes permanent marks and that he has to warn people about before every sale.
A tack and saddle stall with everything from bridles to panniers, staffed by someone who knows horses and who will not sell inappropriate equipment to someone who clearly doesn't.
A vendor of pre-written letters for the illiterate — common occasions, common sentiments, blanks for names — with a scribe available to personalize them on request.
A fishing supply stall with hooks, line, lures, traps, and a copy of a local fishing almanac that may or may not be current.
A bucket and barrel maker selling finished containers and custom-fitting lids to barrels that weren't his, which is the majority of his business.
A stall of regional ceramics, tourist-grade and local-grade mixed, with the vendor willing to point out the difference if asked.
A wool seller with skeins in undyed natural colors and one shelf of dyed yarns from a local dyer, the two in a pricing arrangement that neither discusses.
A lockbox vendor with demonstrations: 'you try to open it, you can't, that's the whole point, yes every one.'
A vendor of small statuary — local saints, local gods, local heroes — who is not religious herself and who finds the market for religious objects a source of reliable income.
A stall of ready-made clothing for travel: cloaks, sturdy trousers, linen shirts, and one rack of things in 'unusual sizes' that turns out to be well-stocked.
A whetstone and edge tool vendor who sharpens on the spot, by hand, to a standard that embarrasses people who thought their tools were sharp.
A pigeon messenger service operating from a stall with a small coop, a rate sheet, and an honest assessment of which routes are reliable and which are not.
A spellbook supplier selling blank books of specific quality — heavy vellum, tight binding, waterproofed — and charging accordingly, no explanation required.
A vendor of salvage goods: things with original context removed — clasps, hardware, decorative elements — sold by weight to jewelers and craftspeople.
A stall selling cooking equipment: pots, pans, trivets, fire irons — all used, all functional, all priced at the level of someone who needs them to work rather than look good.
A seller of travel chests and pack frames, with a chart showing which configuration holds how much of what, and a track record of not exaggerating.
A scroll case vendor whose cases are weatherproofed, padded, and labeled for contents, and who also sells a small catalog of scribes who will write things to put in them.
A honey seller with hive yields from three locations, each with a flavor profile written in his own handwriting on the lid.
A stall of weapons that aren't magical but are very good — the vendor's own work, a two-decade career, a guarantee on every piece.
A bird seller with cages of common species kept as pets, plus one bird that sings in a way locals claim means something, and one bird that definitely does mean something.
A vendor of river sand and fine grit for cleaning and abrasive uses, whose inventory seems mundane until the uses are understood.
A small-scale map stall specializing in building interiors: inns, temples, guildhalls of major cities — produced by someone who has been inside all of them.
A lantern and lamp oil stall with four grades of oil from cheapest to cleanest, plus a portable lantern model the vendor clearly designed himself and improved twice.
A tobacco seller with loose leaf and pressed cake, with a guide to pipes and pipe care that is longer and more technical than most purchasers expect.
A stall of paper — sheets, folded, pressed — in varying quality from accounting-grade to letter-grade to a single ream of something imported that the seller is clearly proud of.
A vendor of musical instrument maintenance supplies: rosin, string, valve oil, luthier glue — and a small hand drum she'll sell if pressed.
A seller of pre-made camp equipment kits: fire steel, tinder box, compass, folding cup, bedroll strap — assembled by someone who has had a bad camp and a good idea.
A dried herb stall with culinary and medicinal sections clearly separated, and a third section the vendor describes as 'use cautiously and not near open flame.'
A wagon repair vendor who keeps a stock of wheels, axle pins, and hardware, doing roadside emergency repairs for travelers who couldn't make it to a proper smithy.
A vendor of cheap printed almanacs: weather predictions, planting guides, tide tables, and a horoscope section the vendor admits is fiction.
A small cosmetics stall with preparations for skin, hair, and nails, sourced locally, priced honestly, and accompanied by demonstrations that are not subtle.
A seller of foreign currency — exchange rates on a board, updated daily — with a secondary service of identifying counterfeit coins for a flat fee.
A military surplus dealer with pre-owned armor, supplies, and equipment from a war that ended recently enough that the gear isn't antique, just available.
A pen and pencil stall where the quality difference between the cheapest and most expensive item is immediately legible to anyone who picks them both up.
A stall selling portable shrines — icons, incense holders, small bells — for travelers who maintain religious observance on the road.
A lard and tallow vendor selling fat for cooking, waterproofing, and a dozen other uses, whose product is unglamorous, essential, and underpriced.
A stall of secondhand maps and journals from previous travelers, some annotated, some worn, one whose previous owner made notes on every page in a system no one has decoded.
A glove seller with work gloves, riding gloves, formal gloves, and one pair of gloves whose purpose he describes only as 'specialty' and recommends for cold climates and sensitive operations.
A vendor of measuring instruments: rulers, scales, astrolabes, simple compasses — practical science at market prices.
A butcher selling cuts from a morning's work, wrapping in paper, pricing by weight, running out by noon and locking up rather than keeping substandard stock.
A pottery stall with common forms — cups, bowls, jars — plus one shelf of someone's artistic work that the vendor is selling on commission for a potter who needs the money.
A charcoal seller operating from the back of a cart, selling by the sack for forge use, cooking use, and the drawing pencils of three regular artists who appear at intervals.
A seller of signal devices: flags, signal mirrors, lantern shutters — the communication equipment of caravans and scouts, priced for the professional market.
A stall of pre-made bandages, wound dressings, and splints, assembled by someone who has seen field medicine and decided the market needed better preparation.
A vendor of regional guides — handwritten, self-published — to six towns within a day's ride, with the author's name on each and a reputation he's built over twenty years.
A stall selling writing desks: portable, folding, sized for field use — the kind of thing a traveling administrator would find essential and most people don't know exists.
A cider and small beer vendor with barrels, selling by the cup and by the jug, whose prices do not change with the time of day or the sobriety of the buyer.
A vendor of belt and baldric hardware: buckles, rings, keepers, D-rings — small metal bits that solve the specific problem of things not staying where you put them.
A dry goods stall selling flour, salt, oats, and dried legumes in whatever quantity the buyer specifies, from a cup to a hundredweight.
A tent and shelter vendor with three sizes, two materials, and one collapsible design that everyone underestimates until they see it assembled.
A secondhand clock and timepiece dealer who can describe the provenance of every item and whose descriptions are always accurate about the mechanism and somewhat embellished about the history.
A stall of animal medicines: salves, powders, and preparations for livestock, with a practitioner available to answer questions between customers.
A vendor of walking staves — simple, graded — plus one staff that's been modified by its previous owner into something more useful than anyone asked for.
A hat seller specializing in travel headwear: sun hats, rain hats, cold weather caps — no vanity hats, by policy, stated on a sign at the front of the stall.
A specialty tea seller from a distant region, working from a small table with samples that draw a crowd of people who have never had tea this way before.
A rope ladder vendor whose product is for builders and farmers but who consistently has customers who don't mention their intended use, and who doesn't ask.
A stall of small animal traps — humane and otherwise — with a printed guide to local pest species and their habits that is more accurate than any official pamphlet.
A vendor of small reagent cases: empty, padded, sized for vials and samples, sold to alchemists, healers, and people who won't say.
A fletching stall with arrow components — shafts, heads, fletches — and a book of regional arrow styles that indicates more about regional history than most history books.
A stall selling dried flowers and pressed plants for decorative and medicinal use, the border between categories unclear and deliberate.
A boot vendor whose stock runs from work boots to riding boots to one pair of boots made for walking quietly, which he keeps under the table and mentions only once per day.
A vendor of personal defense items that aren't weapons: alarm horns, noise-makers, bright powders, and one device of her own design that she will not demonstrate indoors.
A small press printing news sheets: one side current events, one side notices, sold for a copper, updated three days a week, the editor is also the printer is also the vendor.
A stall selling wax — sealing wax in fifteen colors, candle wax, waterproofing wax — with a seal stamp collection the vendor rents by the impression.
A foreign vendor selling things from their home country that are mundane there and exotic here, which is an arbitrage they've built a comfortable life on.

A stall of small specialty tools for specific crafts: engravers, glass cutters, leather stamps, tailoring awls — the equipment of narrow trades, useful to fewer people, more useful to those people than anything else.
A bread seller whose product is still warm, who will be sold out in two hours, and who does not negotiate on price or make change for large denominations out of principle.
A vendor of secondhand musical instruments who can play everything he sells and who gives a brief performance of each to demonstrate its character before quoting a price.
A stall of pre-mixed pigments for artists and signmakers, with custom mixing available, run by someone who has mixed colors for thirty years and who has opinions about every primary.
A veterinary supply stall selling items that also work on humans, as the vendor notes cheerfully when asked.
A vendor of small folded paper goods — bags, envelopes, boxes — who learned paper folding in a far country and whose containers are more structurally sound than they look.
A curiosity stall whose stock changes weekly and whose vendor has no idea what most of it is, accurately, and charges accordingly.

An empty stall, set up perfectly, cloth spread, weight anchors in place, goods organized — but the vendor isn't here and hasn't been, and the stock is clearly worth picking up and walking away with, and someone has left a note saying 'I'll be back in an hour. Please don't.'
